data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-11T01:43:09.053Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically around synchronous LINQ operations.

This is a knowledge question about .NET versioning and EF Core changes. Let me think about what I know:

In EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider, there has been a long-standing trajectory of removing synchronous API support. The Cosmos DB SDK is inherently async, so EF Core's Cosmos provider has been moving toward async-only.

In EF Core 9 (.NET 9), the Cosmos provider started throwing by default when sync APIs were used, but you could opt in to sync-over-async behavior via a warning configuration.


By .NET 10, that sync-over-async opt-in remained available but was increasingly discouraged. Looking ahead to .NET 11, the likely scenario is that the Cosmos provider will completely remove the sync-over-async workaround, meaning synchronous operations like `ToList()`, `First()`, `Count()`, and `SaveChanges()` would throw `InvalidOperationException` with no fallback except converting to async calls. I'm working through the actual EF Core Cosmos timeline more carefully. In EF Core 9, the Cosmos provider started blocking synchronous operations by default, but there was an opt-in mechanism using warning suppression to allow sync-over-async. By EF Core 11 in November 2026, that opt-in would be completely removed, forcing all operations to be fully asynchronous.
